title: Introduction to PLM and the UI
description: Understand product life cycle management concepts, the transition from PDM to PLM, and explore the Windchill user interface including the Home page, Navigator, and workspace customization.

topics.title: What is Product Life Cycle Management?

content.text: Every product follows a life cycle - from the initial spark of an idea through design, manufacturing, and ongoing service. At each stage, teams generate critical data: documents, concept sketches, MCAD and ECAD models, software, work instructions, service procedures, user manuals, and manufacturing processes. The more complex the product, the more complex this generated data becomes. And the longer a product remains in service, the longer that data must be stored, retrieved, updated, and modified.

Product Life Cycle Management (PLM) is the process of managing the complete journey of a product from inception through retirement. PLM ensures that the right data is available to the right people at the right time - across every stage of the product's life. It is the foundation of a digital thread strategy, connecting all product data in a continuous, traceable flow.

rows: Requirements
Define what the product must do and the constraints it must meet
Requirements documents, regulatory specs, concept sketches
Design
Create detailed product definitions using CAD, simulation, and analysis
MCAD/ECAD models, software, specifications, BOMs
Manufacture
Build the product according to design specifications
Work instructions, manufacturing processes, quality records
Service
Maintain, repair, and support the product in the field
Service procedures, user manuals, field change notices

content.text: PLM is the foundation of a digital thread strategy. A digital thread connects every piece of product data - from initial requirements through design, manufacturing, and service - into a single traceable flow, making data available to the right people at the right time.

content.prompt: Arrange the four product life cycle stages in the correct order:

keyTakeaways: Every product follows a life cycle through requirements, design, manufacture, and service
Each stage generates critical data that must be stored, retrieved, and managed over the product's lifetime
PLM manages the complete journey of a product from inception to retirement
PLM is the foundation of a digital thread strategy, connecting product data across all stages

topics.title: From PDM to PLM

content.text: Before PLM, organizations relied on Product Data Management (PDM) systems to manage their design files. PDM works like a digital filing cabinet with tools to simplify work - it controls design files across CAD systems and ECAD tools. However, PDM has a significant limitation: it is document-based, meaning product data is hidden within individual documents rather than being independently accessible and interconnected.

rows: Approach
Document-based - product data lives inside files
Part-centric - data is linked to defined parts (WTParts)
Data Model
Files and folders organized by project
Structured BOM with parts as basic elements
Scope
Controls design files across CAD systems
Manages the entire product life cycle across disciplines
Capabilities
File check-in/check-out, version control, search
Change management, configuration management, traceability, impact analysis
Limitations
Product data hidden within documents
Requires organizational commitment to part-centric processes

content.text: PLM takes a part-centric approach. In Windchill, parts (called WTParts) are the basic building blocks. Once parts are defined, all relevant data - CAD models, documents, requirements - is linked to them, creating a structured Bill of Materials (BOM). This part-centric model enables change and configuration management, business rules enforcement, traceability, and impact analyses. PLM becomes the central repository for the BOM and all downstream modifications.

cards.back: PLM provides BOM capabilities to define the initial product structure. Teams begin organizing parts and assemblies before detailed design work starts.
PLM adds CAD data management alongside BOM. Design engineers create 3D models and associate them with parts, building a rich product definition.
PLM supports supplier management, manufacturing BOMs, and process planning. Part data flows downstream to production without manual re-entry.
PLM manages service BOMs and product configuration. Field teams access accurate, up-to-date information for maintenance and support.

content.text: By moving from PDM to PLM, organizations can reduce the number of disconnected systems they need to manage. PLM consolidates design data, BOM management, change control, and downstream processes into a single platform.

content.prompt: Match each product development stage to how PLM is used:

pairs.right: BOM capabilities to define initial product structure
BOM plus CAD data management for detailed design
Service BOMs and product configuration management

keyTakeaways: PDM is a document-based approach that controls design files but hides product data within documents
PLM uses a part-centric approach with WTParts as basic elements, linking all data to defined parts
PLM enables change management, configuration management, traceability, and impact analysis
PLM spans the entire product development cycle - from early BOM definition through manufacturing and service

topics.title: The Windchill User Interface

content.text: Casey Johnson is a mechanical engineer newly hired at PTC Tools, a company that designs and manufactures power tools. Casey has been assigned to the belt sander production team and is shadowing Mei Cheng, a senior design engineer, to learn the Windchill environment. Let's follow Casey as she explores the key components of the Windchill user interface.

The Windchill user interface is organized around four key areas that you will use constantly: the Home page, the Navigator, Quick Links, and Global Search. The Home page is your starting point - a customizable workspace displaying tables of information relevant to your daily work. The Navigator lets you find and browse files, and can be pinned, expanded, or collapsed. Quick Links provide fast access to commonly used tools, preferences, and the Help system. Global Search (also called simple search) sits in the header for quickly finding objects.

rows: Tasks
Work items assigned to you - action items that need your attention
Updates
Recently changed objects - keeps you informed about modifications
Checked-Out Work
Objects you currently have checked out for editing
Packages
Collaboration with external parties - shared collections of objects
Workspaces
Your private management area for organizing personal work
Discussions
Online forums referencing Windchill objects - team communication threads

content.text: Pin the Navigator panel so it stays expanded while you work. This gives you continuous access to Browse, search, and product navigation without needing to reopen it each time.
You can control which tables are visible on your Home page using the Customize menu. This lets you tailor the Home page to show only the information most relevant to your role and daily workflow.

content.prompt: Match each Windchill UI component to its function:

pairs.right: Customizable starting point with tables for tasks, updates, and work items
Find and browse files, expandable panel with Browse tab and Recent Products
Fast access to commonly used tools, preferences, and the Help system
Header-based search for quickly finding objects across Windchill

keyTakeaways: The Windchill UI is organized around four key areas: Home page, Navigator, Quick Links, and Global Search
The Home page displays customizable tables including Tasks, Updates, Checked-Out Work, Packages, Workspaces, and Discussions
The Customize menu controls which tables are visible on your Home page
The Navigator can be pinned, expanded, or collapsed for convenient browsing

topics.title: Browse the Windchill User Interface

content.text: In this exercise, you will sign in to Windchill, explore the Home page and Navigator, discover the products available to you, and view team members assigned to a product context.

You need access to the Windchill training environment to complete this exercise. When prompted for an application, select Windchill Client. Wait a few minutes for the server to fully initialize before proceeding.

content.title: Browse the Windchill User Interface
content.objective: Sign in to Windchill, explore the Home page and Navigator, discover available products, and view team members.

steps.action: When asked to choose your application, select WindchillClient.
steps.detail: The Windchill Client command window will open. Please wait while Windchill starts.
steps.hint: After a few minutes, the Windchill Client command window will open. Wait for Windchill to fully initialize before proceeding.

steps.action: A Google Chrome browser will open with several sign-in options.
steps.detail: This sign-in page is unique to PTC training. It provides preset user accounts for practice.

steps.action: Select Casey Johnson.
steps.detail: You will be signed in as Casey Johnson (cjohnson/ptc) for all exercises in this course.

steps.action: Click the Quick Links menu.
steps.detail: The Quick Links menu is found in the header of the Windchill UI. It is where you can access commonly used tools, preferences, and information.

steps.action: Click Help > Welcome to Windchill to access a quick reminder of where all the key components are located.
steps.detail: The Welcome window provides an overview of key UI component locations including the Navigator, Global Search, and Quick Links.
steps.hint: Simple search is also referred to as global search.

steps.action: Close the Welcome to Windchill window.
steps.detail: You now have an overview of the key Windchill UI components and their locations.

steps.action: Expand the Navigator panel.
steps.detail: The Navigator lets you find and browse for files and other information stored in Windchill. Click the Navigator bar to expand or collapse the pane.
steps.hint: Once you click outside the Navigator panel, it will collapse; however, you have the option to pin it to keep it expanded for convenient access.

steps.action: Resize the Navigator panel to your liking by dragging the green line on the right edge of the panel in either direction.
steps.detail: Adjust the panel width to your preference for comfortable browsing.

steps.action: Pin the Navigator panel in place by clicking the Pin Navigator icon.
steps.detail: Pinning keeps the Navigator expanded so it does not collapse when you click elsewhere.

steps.action: Select the Browse tab.
steps.detail: The Browse tab in the Navigator provides access to products, libraries, and other contexts.

steps.action: Click the Recent Products icon.
steps.detail: Recent Products shows products you have recently accessed for quick navigation.

steps.action: Click the View All link.
steps.detail: Listed are all the products you have access to as Casey Johnson.

steps.action: Click Belt Sander.
steps.detail: This is the product you are assigned to work with in the training environment.

tasks.title: View the Members of Your Team

steps.action: In the Recent Products pane, expand the Belt Sander product to expose the available navigation options.
steps.detail: When you expand the name of a context, you can click the nodes below it to access pages that display detailed information related to the context.

steps.action: Click the Team link in the Browse pane.
steps.detail: Here you can view the other members of the Belt Sander team.

steps.action: Click the Home icon to return to the Home page.
steps.detail: The Home icon is always available in the header for quick navigation back to your starting point.

steps.action: Unpin the Navigator panel.
steps.detail: Keep the Navigator unpinned when not actively browsing to maximize your workspace area.

steps.action: Keep the Home page open for the next exercise.
steps.detail: You will continue working from the Home page in the next exercise.

keyTakeaways: The Welcome to Windchill window provides a quick overview of the UI layout
The Navigator can be expanded, resized, and pinned for convenient access to products and files
The Browse tab in the Navigator lets you view all products you have access to
The Team page shows members and their roles within a product context

topics.title: Tailor the Home Page

content.text: In this exercise, you will customize your Windchill Home page by adding relevant tables and rearranging them for efficient access to the information you use most often.

content.title: Tailor the Home Page
content.objective: Customize your Windchill Home page by adding relevant tables and rearranging them for efficient access.

tasks.title: Add Tables to Your Home Page

steps.action: Click the Home icon to display your Home page.
steps.detail: The Home page is your starting point in Windchill and the hub for your daily workflow.
steps.hint: You should be signed in to Windchill as cjohnson/ptc.

steps.action: Click Customize.
steps.detail: The Customize menu controls which tables are visible on your Home page.

steps.action: To add tables to the Home page, select the following choices from the list: Tasks, Updates, Checked-Out Work, Packages, Discussions.
steps.detail: A description of the tables is provided at the end of this exercise.
steps.hint: Some of these tables may already be selected.

steps.action: Click outside the list to close it.
steps.detail: The selected tables now appear on your Home page.

steps.action: To move the Checked-Out Work table to the top of the Home page, drag the Checked-Out Work link at the top of the pane from the far-right side to the far-left side of the list.
steps.detail: Moving Checked-Out Work to the top gives you immediate visibility of items you are actively editing.

steps.action: Drag the Updates link from its current position to place it after the Checked-Out Work link.
steps.detail: This places your most time-sensitive information at the top of the page.

steps.action: Click the Tasks link at the top of the page to automatically scroll down and display the My Tasks table.
steps.detail: Clicking a table name in the header scrolls directly to that table on the page.

keyTakeaways: The Customize menu lets you select which tables appear on your Home page
Tables can be rearranged by dragging their links to change the display order
Placing frequently used tables like Checked-Out Work at the top improves daily efficiency
Clicking a table name in the header scrolls directly to that table on the page
